title: Hausted Horizon 462 Series Stretcher - Hydraulic pump weak, drifting, leaking, or not lifting
description: Troubleshooting weak lift, height drift, visible hydraulic leaks, pump pedal issues, or stretcher not raising properly.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the patient from the stretcher if the height adjustment is unstable, drifting, leaking, or not lifting correctly. Expected outcome: The stretcher is empty and safe to inspect. Why it matters: A hydraulic failure can cause unexpected movement, creating a fall or transfer risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Hydraulic Problem
steps.instructions: Operate the lift pedal or pump mechanism with no patient load. Check whether the stretcher raises, lowers, drifts down, binds, or fails to move. Expected outcome: The reported symptom is confirmed or cannot be duplicated. If the stretcher operates normally, document findings and perform a functional check before returning to service.

steps.title: Check for Visible Hydraulic Fluid Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect under the stretcher, around the pump, hydraulic cylinder, hose/fitting areas if visible, and the floor beneath the unit. Look for wetness, oily residue, or dripping fluid. Expected outcome: No hydraulic fluid should be visible. If fluid is leaking, stop troubleshooting, remove the stretcher from service, and send it for repair.

steps.title: Inspect for External Obstructions or Binding
steps.instructions: Check the lift linkage area, frame, pedals, and moving sections for trash, linen, straps, broken accessories, or damage interfering with movement. Expected outcome: The lift mechanism moves freely without rubbing, binding, or obstruction. If an obstruction is found and safely removed, retest the lift function. If resolved, stop.

steps.title: Check Pump Pedal Movement
steps.instructions: Press the hydraulic pump pedal through its normal travel. Confirm it is not loose, bent, jammed, disconnected, or contacting the frame incorrectly. Expected outcome: The pedal should move smoothly and return properly. If the pedal is physically damaged or does not engage the pump, remove the stretcher from service for repair.

steps.title: Check Release Pedal or Lowering Control
steps.instructions: Confirm the lowering/release pedal is not stuck partially engaged. A partially engaged release can cause the stretcher to drift down or fail to raise. Expected outcome: The release control should return fully to the neutral position. If freeing the control resolves the issue, perform several raise/lower cycles and stop if normal operation returns.

steps.title: Test Lift Under No Load
steps.instructions: Raise the stretcher from the lowest to highest position without a patient. Watch for weak pumping, slow rise, uneven movement, or drift after reaching height. Expected outcome: The stretcher should raise smoothly and hold position. If it lifts empty but feels weak, drifts, or requires excessive pumping, suspect hydraulic wear or internal leakage.

steps.title: Check for Height Drift
steps.instructions: Raise the stretcher to a mid or high position and let it sit unloaded for several minutes. Monitor whether it slowly lowers. Expected outcome: The litter should hold height without noticeable drop. If the stretcher drifts down, remove it from service for hydraulic evaluation.

steps.title: Listen and Feel for Abnormal Operation
steps.instructions: During pumping, listen for unusual clicking, grinding, air-like pumping feel, or lack of resistance. Also check for uneven lift action. Expected outcome: Pumping should feel consistent and controlled. If the pump feels soft, inconsistent, or does not build lift pressure, remove the stretcher from service.

steps.title: Inspect for Frame or Lift Component Damage
steps.instructions: Check for bent lift arms, damaged weld areas, missing hardware, loose linkages, or signs the stretcher was overloaded or impacted. Expected outcome: No obvious structural damage should be present. If damage is found, remove the stretcher from service and escalate for repair or replacement evaluation.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Cycling a Leaking or Drifting Stretcher
steps.instructions: If hydraulic leakage, uncontrolled lowering, or failure to hold height is confirmed, stop functional testing. Expected outcome: The stretcher is not used until repaired. Why it matters: Continued operation can worsen the failure and create a patient handling hazard.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Hausted Horizon 462 stretcher would not raise properly and slowly drifted down after pumping.
CCR.Cause: Verified weak hydraulic lift and height drift during unloaded functional testing; no external obstruction found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed stretcher from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for hydraulic pump/lift assembly repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior, if any
Whether accessories, linens, straps, or attachments were removed or repositioned during troubleshooting
Power behavior, if any powered accessories or powered stretcher features were involved
Environmental factors such as wet floors, crowded rooms, uneven surfaces, or signs of impact
Indicator lights, if any powered accessory indicators were present
Whether the stretcher lifted with no load
Whether it drifted down after being raised
Any visible hydraulic fluid or oily residue
Pump pedal condition and travel
Release pedal position and return
Any abnormal noise, soft pedal feel, or binding
Any visible frame, linkage, or lift arm damage
Whether casters were locked during testing
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Hausted Horizon 462 Series Stretcher - Brake/steer system not locking, releasing, or tracking correctly
description: Troubleshooting brake, steer, caster lock, pedal linkage, or tracking issues that affect safe stretcher movement or parking.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the stretcher from patient use if the brake/steer system is unreliable, hard to control, or will not hold position. Transfer the patient to another safe surface before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The stretcher is evaluated without risk of unintended movement. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, stop and document the action.

steps.title: Place the Stretcher on a Flat, Clean Floor
steps.instructions: Test the brake and steer system on a level surface free of debris, cords, thresholds, floor mats, or uneven flooring. Expected outcome: Floor conditions are ruled out as the cause of poor tracking or brake performance. Why it matters: A stretcher may appear to pull, drift, or fail to lock if tested on an uneven or contaminated floor.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Condition
steps.instructions: Cycle the brake/steer pedal through each position: brake, neutral, and steer if equipped. Confirm whether the issue is failure to lock, failure to release, poor tracking, or a pedal that does not fully engage. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified before adjustments or repair decisions are made.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Pedal Obstruction or Incomplete Pedal Travel
steps.instructions: Inspect around the brake/steer pedals for debris, dropped supplies, damaged covers, bent pedal arms, or anything preventing full movement. Expected outcome: Pedal moves fully into each position with a positive feel. If debris or obstruction is found and removed, retest. If normal operation returns, stop.

steps.title: Inspect All Casters and Wheels Externally
steps.instructions: Check for flat spots, missing tread, wrapped string, tape, hair, floor wax buildup, damaged bearings, loose hardware, or uneven wheel movement. Expected outcome: All wheels rotate and swivel freely when released. Why it matters: A caster problem can mimic a brake/steer failure by causing dragging, pulling, or poor tracking.

steps.title: Confirm Brake Engagement at Each Caster
steps.instructions: With the stretcher unloaded and on a flat surface, place the pedal in the brake position and gently attempt to move the stretcher. Observe whether one or more casters still roll or swivel. Expected outcome: The stretcher should resist movement and remain parked. If one caster does not lock while others do, suspect caster brake linkage, adjustment, or caster assembly damage.

steps.title: Confirm Brake Release at Each Caster
steps.instructions: Move the pedal to neutral/released position and push the stretcher forward, backward, and side-to-side. Expected outcome: The stretcher should roll freely without dragging. If a wheel remains partially locked or drags, inspect that caster area for linkage binding, brake pad contact, or wheel damage.

steps.title: Check Steer Function and Tracking
steps.instructions: Place the pedal in steer mode and push the stretcher in a straight path. Confirm whether the designated steer caster locks directionally and whether the stretcher tracks straight without excessive pulling. Expected outcome: The stretcher should track predictably with the steer caster engaged. If steer does not engage or the stretcher wanders, suspect steer lock engagement, caster alignment, worn caster components, or linkage issues.

steps.title: Inspect Visible Brake/Steer Linkage
steps.instructions: Without deep disassembly, look underneath for bent rods, disconnected linkage, missing hardware, loose fasteners, broken springs, or obvious damage. Expected outcome: Linkage should move consistently when the pedal is cycled. If linkage is disconnected, bent, or damaged, remove the stretcher from service for repair.

steps.title: Listen and Feel for Abnormal Engagement
steps.instructions: Cycle the pedal slowly and note grinding, popping, excessive looseness, no resistance, or failure to detent into position. Expected outcome: Pedal should have controlled movement and clear position changes. Why it matters: Abnormal pedal feel often points to mechanical wear or linkage failure rather than user error.

steps.title: Check for Frame or Caster Mount Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect caster mounting points, frame corners, and lower structure for impact damage, cracks, deformation, loose bolts, or signs the stretcher was struck or dropped. Expected outcome: Caster mounts and frame structure should be secure and aligned. If frame or caster mount damage is found, remove from service. Do not return to use until repaired and verified.

steps.title: Perform a Final Functional Check if No Damage Is Found
steps.instructions: Cycle through brake, neutral, and steer several times. Test locking, releasing, turning, and straight tracking on a flat surface. Expected outcome: Brake locks securely, neutral rolls freely, and steer tracks correctly. If the stretcher passes consistently, return to service according to facility policy.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Hausted Horizon 462 Series stretcher brake/steer pedal would not lock the stretcher securely and the unit pulled during transport.
CCR.Cause: Found debris wrapped around one caster and brake engagement inconsistent at the right foot-end caster during functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed debris, inspected caster movement, cycled brake/neutral/steer positions, and verified stretcher locked, released, and tracked correctly before returning to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue involved brake, neutral, steer, or all positions
Which caster failed to lock or release
Whether the stretcher pulled left or right during transport
Floor surface where the issue was reported
Pedal position and feel
Caster condition
Any wrapped debris, buildup, or flat spots
Visible linkage condition
Loose, bent, or missing hardware
Alarm behavior or reported safety concern
Accessories swapped or removed during testing
Power behavior, if any powered accessory or powered stretcher feature was involved
Environmental factors such as flooring, thresholds, cords, mats, wet floors, or debris
Indicator lights, if any powered accessory or related control indicator was involved
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Hausted Horizon 462 Series Stretcher - Side rail latch, rail movement, or rail release failure
description: Side rail will not latch, release, raise, lower, or stay securely locked during normal stretcher use.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the stretcher from patient use if the side rail does not lock securely or moves unexpectedly. Transfer the patient to another safe surface if needed. Expected outcome: No patient remains dependent on an unsafe rail. If this resolves the immediate safety risk, stop and document the action.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Side Rail Issue
steps.instructions: Identify which side rail is affected and what failure is occurring: will not raise, will not lower, will not latch, releases unexpectedly, binds, or feels loose. Expected outcome: The exact symptom and affected rail are confirmed. Why it matters: A latch failure, movement obstruction, and damaged rail assembly may look similar at first.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Damage
steps.instructions: Check the rail, latch handle, release lever, hinge points, pivot brackets, mounting hardware, and surrounding frame area for bending, cracks, missing parts, or impact damage. Expected outcome: No visible damage or missing hardware is found. If damaged components are found, remove the stretcher from service and send for repair.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Rail Path
steps.instructions: Look for bedding, straps, IV lines, patient belongings, debris, tape, or accessory mounts interfering with rail travel or latch engagement. Expected outcome: The rail moves freely through its normal range without anything blocking it. If an obstruction is removed and the rail latches normally, return the stretcher to service only after confirming safe operation.

steps.title: Operate the Rail Slowly Through Its Full Motion
steps.instructions: Raise and lower the rail using normal operation. Do not force it. Listen and feel for binding, grinding, uneven motion, or sticking. Expected outcome: The rail moves smoothly and locks positively in the intended positions. If the rail binds or requires force, remove the stretcher from service.

steps.title: Confirm the Latch Fully Engages
steps.instructions: Raise the side rail into the locked position and gently apply pressure in the expected direction of use. Verify that the latch clicks or seats fully and does not slip. Expected outcome: The rail remains locked and does not partially release. If the latch does not fully engage, the stretcher should not be used.

steps.title: Check the Release Handle or Lever Action
steps.instructions: Operate the release mechanism and confirm it returns to its normal resting position after being released. Look for looseness, sticking, broken plastic, bent linkage, or failure to spring back. Expected outcome: The release mechanism moves normally and returns fully. If the release mechanism sticks or does not return, remove the stretcher from service.

steps.title: Inspect Mounting Hardware and Rail Alignment
steps.instructions: Check for loose fasteners, shifted brackets, or rail misalignment. A rail that is slightly bent or out of alignment may not allow the latch to seat correctly. Expected outcome: Hardware appears secure and the rail lines up correctly with the latch points. Do not continue using the stretcher if alignment or hardware security is questionable.

steps.title: Compare to the Opposite Side Rail
steps.instructions: If the opposite rail is functioning normally, compare latch feel, movement, handle return, rail height, and locking position. Expected outcome: The suspect rail behaves the same as the functioning rail. Why it matters: A side-to-side comparison can quickly reveal abnormal movement or incomplete latch engagement.

steps.title: Perform a Final Safety Check
steps.instructions: After any minor external correction, raise and lower the rail several times and confirm it locks securely every time. Expected outcome: Side rail operation is repeatable, secure, and safe. If the rail passes consistently, document the check and return the stretcher to service.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the left side rail on the Hausted Horizon 462 stretcher would not stay latched in the raised position.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the side rail latch did not fully engage after repeated raise/lower testing, with no bedding or accessory obstruction present.
CCR.Resolution: Removed stretcher from service, labeled Out of Service, and routed for repair evaluation due to unreliable side rail latch operation.

helpfulDetails: Which side rail was affected
Whether the rail would not raise, lower, latch, or release
Whether the latch clicked or failed to seat
Any visible bending, cracks, missing hardware, or loose fasteners
Whether bedding, straps, or accessories were obstructing movement
Whether the release handle returned normally
Whether the opposite rail operated normally
Whether the rail failed during repeated testing
Any alarm behavior or staff-reported safety concern
Any accessories swapped, removed, or found interfering with rail movement
Any power behavior or powered accessory involvement, if applicable
Environmental factors such as tight room spacing, transport damage, wet floors, or repeated impact
Indicator lights or labels present on the stretcher, if applicable
Final device status

title: Hausted Horizon 462 Series Stretcher - Fowler/backrest or Trendelenburg adjustment problem
description: Troubleshooting backrest, Fowler, Trendelenburg, or positioning problems caused by locks, handles, linkages, damage, obstruction, or mechanical failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the stretcher from patient use before troubleshooting any positioning problem. If the stretcher is occupied, transfer the patient to another safe surface before testing adjustments. Expected outcome: The stretcher can be inspected without risk of sudden movement, collapse, or patient injury. If this resolves the concern: Stop and document that the device was not tested during active patient use.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Positioning Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the issue involves the Fowler/backrest, Trendelenburg, reverse Trendelenburg, or more than one positioning function. Operate the control or release handle normally and observe what does or does not move. Expected outcome: The exact failed function is identified. Why it matters: A single-position issue usually points toward a localized handle, linkage, pivot, gas spring, or mechanical support problem.

steps.title: Inspect for Obvious Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Look for bent frame sections, cracked brackets, loose hardware, damaged handles, missing fasteners, broken covers, or signs of impact around the backrest and Trendelenburg mechanism. Expected outcome: No visible damage should prevent movement or safe locking. If damage is found: Remove the stretcher from service and send for repair or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions Around the Moving Sections
steps.instructions: Inspect under and around the litter top, backrest hinge area, Trendelenburg pivot area, side rails, mattress, straps, linens, accessory mounts, and stored items. Remove anything interfering with movement. Expected outcome: The backrest and litter section move freely without binding. If this resolves the issue: Stop and document the obstruction removed.

steps.title: Confirm the Release Handle or Control Moves Normally
steps.instructions: Operate the Fowler/backrest or Trendelenburg release handle while observing whether the handle feels loose, jammed, disconnected, or unusually stiff. Do not force the handle. Expected outcome: The handle should move smoothly and produce a clear mechanical response. If the handle is damaged, loose, or nonresponsive: Remove the stretcher from service for repair.

steps.title: Check Whether the Mechanism Locks and Releases Properly
steps.instructions: Raise and lower the affected section through its normal range only if safe to do so. Confirm that the position holds securely after the handle is released. Expected outcome: The section should move smoothly and lock without drifting, dropping, or slipping. If it will not hold position: Remove from service immediately. A stretcher that cannot hold position is a patient safety risk.

steps.title: Inspect the Backrest Support Area
steps.instructions: For Fowler/backrest issues, inspect the hinge points, backrest frame, support arms, release cable or rod area, and visible mounting points. Look for bent hardware, missing pins, loose fasteners, or asymmetric movement. Expected outcome: Both sides of the backrest should move evenly and remain mechanically secure. If one side binds or shifts differently: Remove from service for repair.

steps.title: Inspect Trendelenburg Movement and Stability
steps.instructions: For Trendelenburg or reverse Trendelenburg complaints, verify that the litter top tilts evenly and that the release mechanism fully engages after positioning. Check for frame interference, uneven motion, or failure to lock. Expected outcome: The stretcher should tilt smoothly and remain stable after adjustment. If the litter top will not lock, tilts unevenly, or drops: Remove from service.

steps.title: Check for Loose or Missing External Hardware
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible bolts, knobs, pins, and brackets related to the affected positioning mechanism. Do not disassemble internal lift or locking components beyond normal external inspection. Expected outcome: No loose or missing hardware should be present. If hardware is missing or structural parts are loose: Tag the stretcher Out of Service.

steps.title: Listen and Feel for Binding, Grinding, or Sudden Release
steps.instructions: Slowly operate the adjustment while listening for grinding, popping, scraping, or sudden movement. Stop immediately if the stretcher drops, catches, or releases unexpectedly. Expected outcome: Movement should be controlled and predictable. If abnormal movement or noise is present: Remove from service for repair.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Final Function Check
steps.instructions: After any simple correction, such as removing an obstruction or repositioning accessories, cycle the affected function several times without a patient. Confirm smooth movement, full locking, and stable support. Expected outcome: The backrest or Trendelenburg function operates normally and holds position securely. If the issue is resolved: Return to service only if the stretcher is stable, safe, and fully functional.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Hausted Horizon 462 Series stretcher backrest would not raise and hold position during setup.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the Fowler release handle moved, but the backrest support would not lock securely after adjustment.
CCR.Resolution: Stretcher was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation of the backrest locking mechanism.

helpfulDetails: Affected function: Fowler/backrest, Trendelenburg, reverse Trendelenburg, or multiple functions
Whether the section moves at all
Whether the section locks and holds position
Any drifting, dropping, binding, or uneven movement
Release handle condition
Visible bent brackets, loose hardware, or missing fasteners
Obstructions removed, if any
Unusual noise, sudden release, or rough movement
Alarm behavior, if any powered accessories or indicators are involved
Accessories swapped or repositioned, including mattress, straps, linens, or accessory mounts
Power behavior, if any powered positioning feature or accessory is involved
Environmental factors, including floor condition, room setup, or storage damage
Indicator lights, if any powered accessory or control indicator is present
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Hausted Horizon 462 Series Stretcher - Casters, Wheels, Bearings, Litter Top, or Accessory Mounts Damaged
description: Troubleshooting damaged stretcher casters, wheels, bearings, litter top surfaces, side accessories, or mounting points affecting safe transport or patient support.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the stretcher from patient use before inspection. If a patient is on the stretcher, transfer the patient to another safe stretcher or bed before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The stretcher is empty, stable, and safe to inspect. If this resolves the immediate safety concern, stop and document the action.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Damage
steps.instructions: Ask staff what was noticed: difficult steering, wheel noise, loose accessory, damaged litter top, wobble, cracked component, or visible breakage. Inspect the area reported. Expected outcome: The complaint is confirmed or narrowed to a specific component. Why it matters: Stretcher complaints are often reported generally, but the issue may be isolated to one caster, mount, rail, mattress deck, or accessory.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Unsafe Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracked caster housings, missing hardware, bent brackets, sharp edges, broken accessory mounts, torn litter top surfaces, exposed padding, or loose parts. Expected outcome: No sharp, loose, or structurally unsafe conditions are present. If any unsafe condition is found, remove the stretcher from service and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect All Casters and Wheels
steps.instructions: Roll the stretcher slowly and observe each wheel. Check for flat spots, embedded debris, cracked tires, missing tread, wobble, or dragging. Expected outcome: All wheels rotate smoothly without binding, wobble, or abnormal noise. If debris is the issue and can be safely removed, retest movement afterward. If normal operation returns, stop.

steps.title: Check Caster Swivel Action
steps.instructions: Turn the stretcher in multiple directions and observe whether each caster swivels freely. Compare all four corners. Expected outcome: Casters swivel smoothly without sticking, grinding, or delayed movement. Why it matters: A caster may roll forward but still fail during turning or tight transport movement.

steps.title: Listen and Feel for Bearing Problems
steps.instructions: Move the stretcher across a clean, flat surface. Listen for grinding, clicking, squealing, or rumbling. Feel for vibration through the frame. Expected outcome: Movement is smooth and quiet with no excessive vibration. If bearing noise or wheel wobble is present, the caster or wheel assembly likely requires replacement or repair.

steps.title: Verify Brake and Steer Interaction
steps.instructions: Confirm that the brake and steer functions are not partially engaged while checking wheel movement. Cycle the brake and steer pedals fully, then retest rolling. Expected outcome: Poor rolling is not caused by a partially engaged brake or steer mechanism. If movement improves after properly cycling the pedals, document and return to service only if all functions test normally.

steps.title: Inspect the Litter Top Surface
steps.instructions: Check the litter top, deck panels, mattress support area, and patient-contact surfaces for cracks, looseness, deformation, missing fasteners, fluid intrusion, or sharp edges. Expected outcome: The litter top is secure, intact, cleanable, and safe for patient support. If the litter top is cracked, unstable, or cannot be properly cleaned, remove the stretcher from service.

steps.title: Check Accessory Mounts and Attachment Points
steps.instructions: Inspect IV pole sockets, oxygen tank holders, side rail mounts, restraint points, accessory brackets, and any attached accessories for looseness, bending, stripped hardware, or damage. Expected outcome: Accessories mount securely without wobble, slippage, or damaged attachment points. If an accessory mount is damaged, do not allow use of that accessory until repaired or replaced.

steps.title: Check for Missing or Loose External Hardware
steps.instructions: Look for missing screws, loose knobs, damaged pins, broken caps, or unsecured covers around the caster assemblies, litter top, side rails, and accessory mounting areas. Expected outcome: External hardware is present and secure. If hardware is loose and can be safely tightened according to facility practice, retest the affected function. If damage remains, escalate.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Transport Function Check
steps.instructions: On a flat surface, roll the stretcher forward, backward, and through turns. Test braking, steering, and accessory stability after inspection. Expected outcome: The stretcher rolls, turns, brakes, steers, and supports accessories safely. If the stretcher passes all checks and no damage remains, document findings and return to service.

steps.title: Do Not Ignore Repeat Damage
steps.instructions: If the same caster, mount, or litter top area repeatedly fails, inspect for environmental causes such as rough thresholds, elevator gaps, overloading, wall impacts, or improper accessory use. Expected outcome: Repeat damage is investigated instead of repeatedly replacing parts without addressing the cause.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported the Hausted Horizon 462 stretcher was difficult to steer and one front caster appeared loose during patient transport.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the front caster wheel had excessive wobble and bearing noise, with no debris or brake engagement causing the issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the stretcher from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for caster assembly replacement and full transport function check.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or staff-reported warning behavior, if any
Accessories swapped, removed, or inspected
Power behavior, if any powered accessories or powered functions are involved
Environmental factors such as rough thresholds, elevator gaps, wall impacts, overloading, or repeated transport route issues
Indicator lights or visible status indicators, if any powered accessories are involved
Which caster or wheel location was affected
Whether the wheel rolled, dragged, wobbled, or made noise
Brake and steer pedal behavior
Any visible caster, fork, bracket, or wheel damage
Litter top condition
Accessory mount or bracket involved
Missing or loose hardware found
Whether debris was removed
Whether the stretcher passed a final roll, steer, and brake check
Final device status
